text: Silver-copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts are one of the most commonly used catalysts in the electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2RR) toward realization of a carbon-neutral society. Despite a large number of studies on the design of AgCu catalysts, very little is known on the evolution behavior of these catalysts, which leads to uncertainty on the active sites of the catalyst. Here, we prepared intermixed and phase-separated AgCu nanoparticles on carbon paper and investigated their structural evolutions in CO2RR process using time-sequential electron microscopy and elemental mapping techniques. Our results demonstrate that the Cu atoms exhibited a large diffusion behavior during the electrochemical process and dissolution of Cu into electrolyte and re-precipitation of Cu particles. The bimposed on the as-prepared intermixed and phase-separated AgCu precursors always tend toward a stable state with the formation of the Ag0.88Cu0.12 and Ag0.05Cu0.95 phases and remain stable with the coexistence of Ag-rich and Cu-rich grains in both the bulk and surface of catalyst. These results confirm that the Ag–Cu phase boundaries play a crucial role in the CO2RR activity. Operando high-energy-resolution X-ray absorption spectroscopy also confirmed that the metallic Cu is active sites in the CO2RR process.
